title: The 7 Hires a Hardware Startup Needs to Get Right | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: a16z's Zabie Elmgren argues that for American Dynamism hardware startups, engineering is table stakes; the functions that actually determine whether a great product becomes a great business are Manufacturing, Deployments, Supply Chain, Finance, Sales, Policy, and Marketing, and she distills how top operators source and interview for each based on interviews with founders at Tesla, Rivian, Hadrian, Base Power, Anduril, Saronic, and Radiant.

insights.claim: Hardware business models are uniquely unforgiving because they involve deploying physical products across a diverse set of stakeholders, which is effectively the definition of an 'American Dynamism' company and raises the stakes of hiring for non-engineering roles far above typical software startups.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating hardware/defense startups should weight non-engineering org quality (finance, supply chain, deployments) as heavily as product/engineering quality when underwriting execution risk.
insights.quote: When deploying physical hardware or dealing with a diverse set of stakeholders, business models become uniquely unforgiving.

insights.claim: Strong engineering is merely table stakes for a hardware startup; whether a great product becomes a great business is decided in seven less-obvious functions: Manufacturing, Deployments, Supply Chain, Finance, Sales, Policy, and Marketing.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Use this as a due-diligence checklist: score hardware startups on these seven functional hires, not just technical talent, before investing.
insights.quote: Strong engineering is table stakes... the engineering feats that take center stage are just the beginning.

insights.claim: The best hires for these critical functions have an instinct for what 'great' looks like from a more established organization, but are not so fanatical about one specific way of doing things that they can't adapt to a startup's culture.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: When assessing a startup's hiring, favor candidates who bring proven process discipline but show adaptability, over rigid big-company transplants.

insights.claim: These seven functional hires compound on each other: a great manufacturing leader shapes what engineering can bring to reality, a great deployment lead closes the feedback loop, a great policy leader shapes the roadmap through regulatory requirements, and a great marketing hire makes every other role easier to fill.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Compounding effects mean sequencing these hires correctly early creates outsized long-term advantage; delayed or wrong hires in any one function drags down the others.
insights.quote: These foundational hires compound. A great manufacturing leader shapes what engineering gets to bring to reality.

insights.claim: Tesla staffed its Texas 4680 battery facility not from other battery or automotive companies but from high-speed bottling plants and syringe manufacturing facilities, because the US had no existing pool of battery manufacturing operators.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: For reindustrializing hardware startups, source manufacturing talent by underlying process skill (high-volume, high-spec production) rather than by industry label; look at consumer packaged goods and other never-offshored sectors.
insights.quote: When we were building the 4680 facility in Texas, there weren’t a lot of battery operations in the United States, so you have to look for analogs. We were hiring people out of high-speed bottling plants, out of syringe manufacturing facilities.

insights.claim: Tesla's Texas 4680 battery plant was a 50 gigawatt-hour facility built in eleven months inside a former JC Penney warehouse, illustrating the speed at which reindustrialized US manufacturing can scale when staffed correctly.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Use this as a benchmark for how fast a well-staffed greenfield US battery/hardware facility can be stood up.

insights.claim: Decades of offshoring have created a genuine shortage of US manufacturing talent, and companies like Hadrian are addressing this not by replacing humans with AI but by building software and automation that makes it easier to hire the limited workers who are available.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Frame AI-driven factory automation (e.g., Hadrian) as a labor-supply-constraint solution rather than a headcount-reduction play when evaluating industrial automation startups.

insights.claim: Hadrian's Factory 4 opened in Cherokee, Alabama in March 2026 and is expected to create 1,000 high-paying manufacturing jobs once fully operational.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable milestone to watch for validating Hadrian's factory-automation thesis and regional job creation claims.

insights.claim: The best way to test a manufacturing hire's process-discipline instinct is to ask them to describe a project they completed (or could have completed) with half the time, half the budget, and half the people it normally required.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A concrete interview heuristic operators can reuse when vetting manufacturing leadership hires.

insights.claim: The deployment hire's job is not to manage a process but to own the outcome no matter what goes wrong, in front of customers; most founders hire for this role too late, only after the first deployment fails.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Startups should hire a deployment lead proactively, once the first ten deployments are visible on the horizon, not reactively after a customer-facing failure.
insights.quote: The deployment hire isn’t someone who just manages a process. It’s someone who owns the outcome, whatever it takes to get there.

insights.claim: The optimal deployment-team structure pairs fast-moving 'hustlers' with a technical expert who has already seen the specific product fail in the field, since high-volume deployments should be treated as an extension of the manufacturing line.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A reusable org-design template for hardware startups scaling field deployments.

insights.claim: Dana Paz joined Base Power's deployment function from Anduril's manufacturing team knowing almost nothing about energy, and built a strong team by recruiting new-graduate engineers, SpaceX production coordinators, and military veterans whose experience in high-stakes, low-resource environments translated directly to field deployment work.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Cross-industry hiring (energy company recruiting from defense/aerospace manufacturing and the military) can be a repeatable source of strong operational deployment talent for hardware startups.

insights.claim: In defense specifically, the deployment function takes the form of a lower-volume forward-deployed engineering (FDE) team whose in-field presence with customers builds trust and surfaces real operational requirements that never survive the bureaucratic chain.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Defense hardware investors should check whether portfolio companies have an FDE-style function embedded with customers, as a proxy for real requirements feedback and mission trust.

insights.claim: Rivian lost two weeks of production because of a defective coating on a washer inside a motor assembly, traced to a tier-five supplier in India that Rivian didn't even know existed, illustrating that the supply-chain risk that halts production is almost never the one being actively watched.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Deep supply-chain mapping (beyond tier-one/tier-two) is a material risk factor for hardware manufacturers; investors should ask portfolio companies how far down their supply chain visibility actually extends.
insights.quote: We lost two weeks of production because of a coating on a washer inside a motor assembly — a tier-five supplier in India that we didn’t even know existed.

insights.claim: The core supply-chain hiring challenge is really a sales problem: convincing suppliers to prioritize a startup that isn't yet spending enough money for them to care, which requires a hybrid negotiator, technical translator, and program manager.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Evaluate supply-chain leader candidates on negotiation and relationship-building skill, not just logistics/operations experience.

insights.claim: Rivian ran its R1 vehicle program with over 600 suppliers requiring overwhelming coordination, then deliberately consolidated its supplier base for R2, accepting more concentration risk in exchange for reduced complexity — a judgment call the article calls one of the highest-leverage decisions a supply chain leader makes.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Track supplier count and concentration trends across vehicle/hardware generations as a signal of supply-chain maturity and risk trade-offs at scaling manufacturers.

insights.claim: Jesse Stevens, Base Power's supply chain leader, came from Tesla by way of Duke Energy, combining fast-paced startup manufacturing culture with experience running supply chains at utility scale.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A hybrid Tesla-plus-utility background is a repeatable sourcing pattern for energy-hardware supply chain leadership.

insights.claim: The best way to evaluate supply chain candidates is with scenario-based interviews (a sudden tariff shift, a tier-one plant burning down, a supplier failing to ramp) and to check whether they're already tracking real-world supply chain events unprompted, out of genuine obsession rather than interview prep.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A concrete interview technique for vetting supply chain leadership hires under real-world stress scenarios.

insights.claim: Hadrian's founder argues that fully half of the value hardware companies create over the next decade will come from capital optimization, meaning the finance leader must be a true partner to the founder rather than a scorekeeper, understanding the factory floor as well as the cap table.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Weight capital-allocation sophistication heavily when assessing hardware CFOs; it may be as important to long-run returns as manufacturing execution itself.
insights.quote: Fifty percent of the value we create over the next decade is going to be capital optimization.

insights.claim: Hadrian's CFO, West Owens, spent decades as a financial operator in energy — including as CFO of TerraWatt Infrastructure and VP of Structured Finance at SolarCity — building experience managing capital through genuine adversity, not just growth, which is rarer than growth-only finance experience.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: When sourcing hardware CFOs, prioritize candidates with experience managing capital through volatility/downturns (e.g., prior energy-sector roles), not just growth-stage finance experience.

insights.claim: Finance is structurally harder for hardware companies than software because a fixed-price hardware contract locks in cost assumptions for five to ten years, leaving no ability to reprice, cut costs, or reallocate people the way software companies can.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Underwrite hardware companies' fixed-price contracts with extra scrutiny on cost-assumption durability, since mispricing can't be corrected mid-contract the way software pricing can.

insights.claim: Rivian's CEO frames the first finance hire as a choice between an 'architectural CFO' who thinks in strategic capital allocation and an 'operational controller' who drives consistent monthly execution — and the first hire is more often the latter, sourced from trading floors, private equity, or a strong finance org, before the company eventually needs the former.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Sequence hardware finance hiring: bring in an execution-focused controller first for cost/audit control, and only later hire a strategic architectural CFO once the capital story is legible.

insights.claim: Early hardware startups run almost entirely on venture capital until a second or third factory (F2/F3) proves the production line can be repeated; only then does the financial profile become legible enough to attract debt, non-dilutive funding, or project-level financing.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Investors should expect hardware companies' capital stack to stay venture-dominated until the second/third factory is running, and treat earlier claims of diversified financing skeptically.

insights.claim: Brian Roberts, an a16z GP and former CFO of Lyft and Splunk, identifies five key dimensions of a strong CFO: leadership presence, long-game capital allocation, founder-like intensity, the ability to distill complexity, and standing as a true peer to the CEO.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A reusable five-point CFO evaluation rubric for hardware and non-hardware companies alike.

insights.claim: Rivian's current CFO, Claire Rauh McDonough, came from traditional banking with deep capital markets experience but little operational background, illustrating how the archetype needed for the CFO role shifts from operational to architectural as a hardware company matures.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Expect and plan for a CFO archetype transition (operational to architectural/capital-markets) as a hardware company scales toward maturity.

insights.claim: The fastest path to winning a defense contract starts not with the most senior relationship in the building but with identifying the right program office at the right moment in the budget cycle, six to twelve months before the RFP drops.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Judge defense-startup sales pipelines by whether they're engaging program offices well ahead of RFP release, not just by counting senior relationships.
insights.quote: The fastest path to a defense contract doesn’t always start with the most senior relationship in the building. It starts with identifying the right program office at the right moment in the budget cycle.

insights.claim: Palantir and Anduril's success in breaking down government and enterprise sales barriers has made the path to defense contracts much more legible for later entrants, letting newer defense startups run leaner sales teams that spend less time educating customers — but the primes still capture the lion's share of Department of War revenue.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Don't assume 'Anduril/Palantir playbook' success is fully repeatable at scale for new entrants: the revenue pool is still dominated by legacy primes even as sales friction eases.

insights.claim: Scott Sanders, one of Anduril's earliest sales and business development hires, believes founders must run the sales function themselves until the motion is working, because you cannot evaluate whether a hire is doing the job well until you've done it yourself.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Flag as a red flag any early-stage defense-tech founder who has fully delegated sales before proving the motion themselves.

insights.claim: Government sales for defense hardware breaks into four distinct activities that rarely live in one person: relationships with budget holders, relationships with the acquisition arm, relationships with end users, and technical proposal writing.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Map a defense startup's sales org against these four functions to spot coverage gaps before they show up as lost contracts.

insights.claim: Saronic's co-founder warns that people with impressive government titles often have zero budgetary authority — they can describe the problem but can't buy the solution — making it critical to distinguish budget holders, the acquisition arm, and end users.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Coach defense-tech sales hires to qualify contacts by actual budget authority, not title prestige, to avoid wasted sales cycles.
insights.quote: People think getting in front of individuals with big titles is the win. But they might have zero budgetary authority. They can tell you the problem, but they can’t buy the solution.

insights.claim: Saronic's Navy contracts are run through a hire who came straight out of NAVSEA, reflecting that mature defense sales orgs need dedicated proposal managers and contracting specialists who know a specific customer's (SOCOM, NAVSEA, Air Force) unique process, since each runs its own.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: For defense-tech investors, check whether a portfolio company has agency-specific contracting expertise (e.g., ex-NAVSEA) rather than generalist government-sales staff.

insights.claim: Radiant's president argues the hardest policy hire to find speaks both technical and Washington fluently — able to explain what the product actually does while presenting it without scaring regulators — and that being completely right about the technology isn't enough if you lose the room.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Screen policy hires for bilingual technical/political fluency, not pure subject-matter or pure lobbying expertise.
insights.quote: The hardest thing to find in a policy hire is someone who can speak both languages fluently. Technical enough to understand what the product actually does. Washington enough to know how to present it without scaring people.

insights.claim: Whether the policy function sits under Legal or under go-to-market depends on a simple test: if a policy failure could kill the company (common for energy and public-safety companies), it sits under Legal; if policy's primary value is tactical partnerships and access (common for defense companies), it sits under go-to-market.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Use this as an org-design test when evaluating a regulated hardware startup's structure — a mismatch (e.g., existential regulatory risk sitting under GTM) is a governance red flag.

insights.claim: Saronic runs policy under both functions: under go-to-market it looks like lobbying, budget, and relationship work, while under Legal the team does slower, technical work such as securing DHS waivers so an autonomous cargo ship can sail from Puerto Rico to Houston, or working with the Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) to shape industrial-base tech policy.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Autonomous maritime/defense hardware companies need dual-track policy teams (GTM lobbying plus Legal regulatory waivers) to actually operate across jurisdictions — a capability worth diligencing directly.

insights.claim: Three of Radiant's five executives hold PhDs, reflecting that the intellectual firepower needed to earn the respect of government regulators and technical experts is itself a credibility investment, not just a nice-to-have.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: For nuclear/heavily-regulated hardware startups, executive-team academic credentials can be a meaningful proxy for regulatory credibility and deal-closing ability.

insights.claim: Rita Baranwal, Radiant's Chief Nuclear Officer, learned that the earliest and hardest policy lesson wasn't about the regulations themselves (which she knew well) but about how to present the technology to regulators without overwhelming or alarming them.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Even deeply technical policy experts need explicit coaching on framing/communication, not just subject-matter mastery, to succeed with regulators.

insights.claim: Anduril's communications lead argues that defense companies fail at communications when they treat complexity as an excuse for vagueness; the job is to make an opaque business clear enough that the right customer, hire, and regulator all reach the same conclusion independently.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Judge defense/hardware startup marketing not on polish but on whether messaging achieves clarity across three very different audiences at once (customers, talent, regulators).
insights.quote: Defense companies fail at communications when they assume complexity is an excuse for vagueness.

insights.claim: Base Power's head of growth and marketing, Cole Jones, came from SpaceX where he went from ops engineer to senior product growth manager, never having held a traditional marketing role, illustrating that high-slope early marketing hires often come from outside traditional marketing entirely.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: When sourcing early growth/marketing leaders for hardware startups, prioritize operationally-steeped internal transplants over marketing-credentialed outside hires.

insights.claim: American Dynamism companies should hire marketing talent from outside the industry they're building in — Anduril hired its VP of Communications from Hulu and Mozilla, and its VP of Marketing from PepsiCo and Snap — because messaging drawn from consumer backgrounds avoids sounding invisible like every other company in the sector.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Treat consumer-marketing backgrounds (rather than defense/industrial marketing pedigree) as a positive signal for hardware/defense startup marketing hires.
insights.quote: Turns out consumer companies spike in Marketing for a reason!

insights.claim: Anduril's 'Don't Work at Anduril' recruiting campaign succeeded not because it was clever but because it was honest — a blunt message that didn't smooth over the hard parts of the job — and it deliberately repelled the candidates Anduril didn't want.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: Recruiting campaigns that self-select out weak-fit candidates by naming the job's hard parts can be more effective than polished aspirational messaging.

insights.claim: Base Power's recruiting campaign, 'Don't tell your grandkids all you did was B2B SaaS,' works by directly naming the comfortable alternative a candidate is choosing against, without overselling the pitch.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A reusable recruiting-marketing pattern: name the candidate's comfortable status-quo alternative explicitly rather than only selling the mission.
insights.quote: Don’t tell your grandkids all you did was B2B SaaS.

insights.claim: The best interview screen for a marketing hire is asking which competitor's marketing is completely wrong: a strong answer names names and identifies specific missed audiences fast, while a candidate who hedges or can't answer is signaling a lack of conviction.
insights.speaker: Zabie Elmgren
insights.so_what: A concrete, reusable interview heuristic for screening marketing/comms leadership candidates for genuine point of view.
